title: Surgery (Craniotomy)
body: A surgeon opens the skull to remove some or all of the tumor. This is the most common treatment when a meningioma is growing, large, or causing symptoms. Many patients recover well after surgery.

title: Stereotactic Radiosurgery
body: A precise form of radiation — like Gamma Knife or CyberKnife — that targets the tumor without cutting. It is often used when the tumor is small or in a hard-to-reach area of the brain.

title: Radiation Therapy
body: Focused beams of high-energy radiation are used to shrink the tumor or stop it from growing. It is often used after surgery if the whole tumor could not be removed.

title: Watchful Waiting
body: If the tumor is small and not causing symptoms, doctors may monitor it with regular MRI scans instead of treating it right away. This approach is common for slow-growing meningiomas.

title: MRI Monitoring
body: Regular brain imaging scans help doctors track the size and growth of the tumor over time — whether before treatment, after surgery, or as part of a watchful waiting plan.

title: Medication & Hormone Therapy
body: Some medications may help control symptoms like swelling or seizures. Because meningiomas can be hormone-sensitive, hormone therapy is sometimes explored as part of treatment.
